title: Robot-as-a-Service (RaaS) for Humanoid Robots
description: How robot-as-a-service (RaaS) works for humanoid robots: subscription vs buying, what's bundled, pricing models, and the pros and cons of each.
quickAnswer: Robot-as-a-service (RaaS) is a subscription model where you pay a recurring fee to use a humanoid robot instead of buying it outright. The fee usually bundles the hardware, software, support, maintenance and updates, turning a large upfront capital cost into a predictable operating cost and shifting uptime and obsolescence risk to the provider. RaaS suits many first deployments; outright purchase can be cheaper over a long, intensive lifespan.

body.text: With robot-as-a-service, you do not buy a machine — you subscribe to a working capability. The provider keeps ownership of the robot and stays responsible for it across its life: uptime, software updates, servicing and eventual replacement. It is the same shift that moved software from boxed purchases to subscriptions, applied to physical robots.

items: The AI software and task configuration
Software updates and improvements over time
Support, and often uptime or performance commitments

body.text: There are two common structures, sometimes combined:

rows: A fixed fee per period — for example monthly — for access to the robot.
A fee tied to output, such as per task, pick or unit of work performed.
Low — spread into a recurring fee
Higher if used intensively for years

runs: RaaS mainly changes the shape of the cost, not the underlying test. Whichever model you choose, the decision still rests on

runs.text: total cost of ownership and ROI

runs: RaaS is attractive for a first deployment: it lowers the barrier to entry, removes maintenance hassle, and lets you scale up or down as needs change — useful while the technology is advancing quickly. Outright purchase can win when you will run a proven robot intensively for many years. For most UK manufacturers starting out, a subscription that bundles support pairs naturally with a staged

faq.question: Is robot-as-a-service cheaper than buying a humanoid robot?
faq.answer: It depends on usage. RaaS lowers upfront cost and bundles support, which suits many first deployments, but an outright purchase can be cheaper over a long, intensive lifespan. Compare total cost of ownership for your specific task and time horizon rather than headline figures.

faq.question: What is included in a RaaS subscription?
faq.answer: Typically the robot hardware, AI software, task configuration, maintenance and spares, ongoing updates, and support — often with uptime or performance commitments. The exact bundle varies by provider, so confirm what is and isn't covered before signing.

faq.question: Does RaaS make it easier to change platforms later?
faq.answer: Generally yes — because you are not locked into owning hardware, RaaS can make it easier to adjust or switch as the technology evolves, especially with a platform-agnostic provider. Check contract length and exit terms to be sure.
